title: Cat Eyes Darting Side to Side: Understanding Feline Nystagmus
meta_desc: Is your cat's eyes darting rapidly? Learn about feline nystagmus, the urgency of care, and how to help your pet through a vestibular episode.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rapid eye movement in cats, known as nystagmus, typically signals a vestibular system issue and requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours, or immediate emergency care if severe symptoms like seizures occur.

What causes rapid, involuntary eye movement in cats?

Rapid, involuntary eye movement in cats is medically known as nystagmus. It typically manifests as the eyes flickering back and forth, up and down, or in a circular motion. This is usually a symptom of a disturbance in the feline vestibular system, which is responsible for maintaining balance and spatial orientation. It is often accompanied by a head tilt, stumbling, or general disorientation.

Is rapid eye movement in my cat a medical emergency?

The urgency level for rapid eye movement is Medium. While darting eyes are rarely immediately life-threatening, they indicate a significant neurological or inner-ear issue that requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if nystagmus is accompanied by:

  • A complete inability to stand
  • Seizures
  • Persistent vomiting

Why should I take a video of my cat’s eye movements for the vet?

Capturing a short video of your cat's eye movements is one of the most helpful things you can do for your veterinarian for the following reasons:

  • Vestibular signs can be intermittent or change based on the cat's position.
  • A clear visual record allows the vet to determine the direction and speed of the eye movement.
  • This specific information is a vital clue in differentiating between a peripheral issue, like a middle ear infection, or a central issue involving the brain.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, nystagmus, characterized by involuntary, rhythmic eye movements, can be a sign of underlying neurological or vestibular dysfunction. Vestibular lesions can manifest as spontaneous positional, horizontal, or rotary nystagmus, often accompanied by dysequilibrium and head tilt. Nystagmus can also be a neurological sign associated with conditions such as Feline Infectious Peritonitis (FIP), where it may occur alongside ataxia and seizures due to CNS involvement. The presence and characteristics of nystagmus (direction, speed, presence of fast/slow phases) are important factors in determining the location and nature of the underlying disorder.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1216)

faqs.question: Is my cat dizzy when their eyes move like this?
faqs.answer: Yes, nystagmus is almost always accompanied by a sensation of vertigo or dizziness, which is why your cat may seem uncoordinated or nauseated.

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause rapid eye movements?
faqs.answer: Yes, deep inner ear infections are a common cause of nystagmus because they interfere with the nerves responsible for balance.

faqs.question: Will my cat's eyes go back to moving normally?
faqs.answer: In many cases, yes. Once the underlying cause—such as inflammation or infection—is treated, the eye movements typically stabilize and return to normal.
